title: Minion Hunter (a Dark Conspiracy game) - RetroLook (w/ the Minion Nation expansion)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lr1GJQXbj4c

Daniel (@dungeondive) digs into Minion Hunter, Lester Smith's 1992 GDW board game set in the Dark Conspiracy world (and its bare-bones Minion Nation expansion — a single cardboard sheet of tables and cut-it-yourself cards). He pegs it as a simplified mix of Talisman and Arkham Horror: an early co-op where players are monster-hunters in a supernatural post-apocalyptic America, racing to thwart the plots of four evil factions (ETs, Fay, Morlocks, Nuke Kids) before any climbs the threat track to 20.

He walks a long sample game: build stats (empathy, stalking, combat, contacts) on an outer "career track," drop into a map of America to confront plots via roll-under d10 checks, and weather demon-ground, outlaw and Proto-Dimension encounters, with a brutal early difficulty that snowballs once money arrives. His core criticism: it's dry — almost no flavour text, so you must bring the theme yourself. He had it set up all week and enjoyed it anyway, and openly wishes a studio like Restoration Games would reprint it: keep the fast, simple core, add art and thematic spice. Charming, brisk, and begging for a glow-up.

----

Daniel enjoyed Minion Hunter despite its near-total lack of flavour text, supplying the theme himself. Can you fall for a mechanically fun but "dry" game, or does thin theming kill it for you?
